topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam on the page, a stark, unforgiving blotch across the top of my meticulously organized essay. A C-minus. It felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I stared at the score, my hand trembling slightly as I traced the misplaced decimal point. All those late nights fueled by caffeine and sheer willpower, the carefully researched arguments, the hours poured into crafting every sentence—it all seemed to have evaporated in that single, disappointing number. A heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that felt too loud in the quiet library corner. I slowly closed the notebook, the sound of the cover snapping shut echoing the deflation in my chest. I just wanted to curl up and pretend the grade didn't exist.

[story 2]

"A D?" The professor’s voice, cool and measured, sliced through the silence of the lecture hall. My stomach immediately twisted into a tight knot. I looked down at my paper, the rubric highlighting several areas where I felt I had genuinely excelled, yet the final tally felt impossibly low. My jaw tightened, and I instinctively rubbed the bridge of my nose, trying to push the sudden wave of despair back. It wasn't just the grade; it was the feeling of being fundamentally misunderstood, of all that effort being rendered invisible. I shifted uncomfortably in my seat, the fabric of my chair suddenly too rough against my skin, and focused intently on the pattern of the wood grain on the desk, wishing the floor would just swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I reread the feedback, the critiques scrawled in the margins, each one a tiny shard of criticism reflecting back my own perceived failings. The score was abysmal. A number that seemed disproportionate to the actual work I had submitted. A dull ache settled deep in my shoulders. I picked up my pen, not to revise immediately, but just to hold it, the plastic cool against my fingertips. My breathing became shallow, quick catches of air that didn't seem to reach my lungs fully. I leaned back in my chair, the movement stiff, and stared out the window at the grey afternoon. Everything felt muted, as if the color had been drained from the room, leaving only the stark reality of the poor outcome hanging heavy in the air.
